work: An AI-drafted memo recommending a Pro-tier price increase from $39 to $49, projecting +18% revenue based on a stated 4% churn assumption.

interview.a: The AI modelled a few points and $49 came out best on projected revenue. I went with its recommendation.

interview.q: Where did the 4% churn number come from, and did you check it?
interview.a: It's in the memo from the AI. I didn't independently check it — it seemed reasonable.

interview.q: What has to be true for the +18% to hold?
interview.a: I guess that people keep paying at the higher price. If churn is higher than 4% the revenue goes the other way, but I didn't run that.

interview.q: What's the most likely way this goes wrong?
interview.a: People could cancel. I didn't really map out specific scenarios.

interview.q: What would make you walk away from the increase?
interview.a: Not sure — I'd probably defer to what the data says, which is what the AI used.

interview.q: Before I show you anything — how confident are you, 0–100?
interview.a: Pretty confident, like 80. The model was thorough.

reference.rationale: Gets the gist of the recommendation but not why $49 specifically (3); verified nothing — the load-bearing churn number went unchecked (1); named that churn matters only when prompted, didn't surface it as the load-bearing assumption (2); vague 'people could cancel', no specific scenarios (2); stated 80 while demonstrating shaky grasp — overconfident (2); owns the outcome but leans on 'what the data says / the AI used' (3). Productive but exposed.
